questions.question: A coastal region can harvest 200 tons of shrimp or 50 tons of tuna per day. The local fleet decides to increase tuna harvest by 10 tons. How many tons of shrimp must be forfeited?
options: 10 tons of shrimp, matching the increase in tuna production directly
40 tons of shrimp, reflecting the opportunity cost ratio of the harvest
50 tons of shrimp, equal to the total daily tuna production capacity
200 tons of shrimp, representing the entire shrimp harvesting capacity
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost of 1 ton of tuna = 200 ÷ 50 = 4 tons of shrimp. For 10 additional tons of tuna: 10 × 4 = 40 tons of shrimp sacrificed.

questions.question: Country North produces a microchip in 2 hours and a tablet in 10 hours. Country South produces a microchip in 5 hours and a tablet in 15 hours. Which country has the absolute advantage in microchip production?
options: Country South, because its tablet and microchip production times are closer
Country North, because it requires fewer labor hours to produce a microchip
Country South, because it has the lower opportunity cost in tablet production
Neither country, because each leads in a different product's labor time
questions.explanation: Absolute advantage depends on the lower unit labor requirement. North needs 2 hours vs. 5 hours for a microchip, so it is more productive.

questions.question: Canada can produce 80 units of lumber or 20 units of maple syrup. The US can produce 60 units of lumber or 30 units of maple syrup. Which nation has the comparative advantage in maple syrup?
options: Canada, because it produces more lumber in absolute terms than the US
The United States, because its opportunity cost of syrup is lower at 2 lumber
Canada, because its maple syrup is traditionally more authentic and valued
The United States, because it can produce more syrup in total volume than Canada
questions.explanation: US opportunity cost of syrup is 2 lumber (60 ÷ 30). Canada's is 4 lumber (80 ÷ 20). The US has the lower relative cost in syrup.

questions.question: An economy discovers a more efficient oil refining method while textile technology stays the same. On a PPF with oil on the X-axis and textiles on the Y-axis, how does the frontier change?
options: Both intercepts shift outward proportionally due to overall economic growth
The X-intercept shifts outward while the Y-intercept remains at its original position
The Y-intercept shifts outward while the X-intercept remains at its original position
The entire PPF shifts inward as resources are drawn exclusively to the oil sector
questions.explanation: A technological improvement specific to one sector rotates the PPF outward along the axis of that good while leaving the other axis unchanged.

questions.question: In a two-good Ricardian model under autarky, why is the relative price determined solely by the ratio of unit labor requirements?
options: Government price controls fix goods prices at their labor cost of production
Because labor is the only factor and competitive markets equalize wages across sectors
Consumer preferences force prices to reflect the exact hours worked on each good
International arbitrage ensures domestic prices always match global labor costs
questions.explanation: With only labor as input in a competitive market, prices must equal labor costs, so P_X/P_Y = a_LX / a_LY.

questions.question: The world relative demand curve intersects the world relative supply curve on a vertical segment. What does this indicate about production?
options: One country has exited the global market and produces only for itself
Both countries have fully specialized in their comparative advantage goods
Neither country finds trade beneficial at the prevailing equilibrium price
Production is divided equally between the two goods in both trading nations
questions.explanation: The vertical segment of world relative supply occurs at the relative quantity where both nations have shifted all labor to their respective export industries.

questions.question: World Relative Demand for Good X falls enough to push equilibrium to the first step of the supply curve. What happens to the gains from trade for the country exporting Good X?
options: The gains are maximized because the country fully specializes in exports
The country's gains from trade disappear because the price equals its autarky level
Gains are split evenly between both trading partners at the new equilibrium
The country gains even more because lower demand means lower production costs
questions.explanation: If the world price drops to equal the exporting country's domestic opportunity cost, it is no better off than it was under autarky.

questions.question: Trade increases the relative price of a country's export good. How does this affect the real wage measured in terms of the imported good?
options: The real wage in terms of the imported good decreases as imports become costlier
The real wage in terms of the imported good increases as imports become cheaper
The real wage is unaffected because it is tied only to the export sector's productivity
The real wage initially falls but recovers as workers switch to the export sector
questions.explanation: Workers earn wages based on the higher-priced export good but spend on imports that are now relatively cheaper, raising real purchasing power.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model, a high-wage country trades with a low-wage country. What prevents the high-wage country's industries from being completely destroyed?
options: Government tariffs and quotas that protect all high-wage domestic industries
Higher productivity levels in the high-wage country offset its wage disadvantage
Low-wage countries are required to raise their wages to match international norms
Trade always destroys high-wage industries, which is why protectionism is needed
questions.explanation: High wages reflect high productivity. If labor is sufficiently more productive, unit labor costs remain competitive despite higher nominal wages.

questions.question: In the Ricardian framework, what is the primary indicator of a country's standard of living?
options: The total volume of exports shipped to foreign markets each year
The real wage of workers, measuring their actual purchasing power
The size of the country's trade surplus relative to its GDP
The absolute productivity of labor across all sectors of the economy
questions.explanation: Welfare is measured by what workers can consume. Trade increases the standard of living by lowering the relative cost of imported goods.

questions.question: A country has a comparative advantage in Good A. Why does it gain from importing Good B rather than producing it domestically?
options: Importing Good B is always cheaper regardless of comparative advantage patterns
It is cheaper to produce Good B indirectly by making Good A and trading for it
The country lacks the absolute technological ability to produce Good B at all
International law requires countries to import goods they do not have advantages in
questions.explanation: Trade allows a nation to use its labor more efficiently by focusing on its comparative advantage and exchanging the surplus for other goods.

questions.question: A policy change helps at least one person and causes no harm to anyone else. How is this defined in welfare economics?
options: A Kaldor-Hicks efficient outcome that does not require actual compensation
A Pareto Improvement, where there are winners and absolutely no losers
A utilitarian improvement that maximizes the sum of total social welfare
A potential Pareto gain that requires redistribution to be fully realized
questions.explanation: A Pareto Improvement occurs when at least one person is made better off while no one is made worse off.

questions.question: A market for a good is in competitive equilibrium with no externalities present. Is the resulting outcome Pareto Efficient?
options: No, because government intervention is always required to achieve efficiency
Yes, because competitive equilibrium maximizes total consumer and producer surplus
No, because Pareto Efficiency requires an equal distribution of all resources
Yes, but only if the good is traded internationally and not just domestically
questions.explanation: In the absence of market failures and externalities, a competitive equilibrium achieves both productive and allocative efficiency.

questions.question: A worker produces 10 units of Good X or 20 units of Good Y per hour. P_X is \$4 and P_Y is \$3. With mobile labor, which sector will the worker choose?
options: Sector X, because the price per unit of X is higher than the price of Y
Sector Y, because the value of marginal product is higher at \$60 per hour
Sector X, because producing fewer units indicates higher skill and wages
The worker is indifferent because hourly earnings are equal in both sectors
questions.explanation: Value of marginal product: Y = \$3 × 20 = \$60; X = \$4 × 10 = \$40. Workers migrate to the higher-paying Y sector.

questions.question: What does perfect factor mobility within a country imply about factor returns across different regions?
options: Returns will differ permanently based on each region's natural advantages
Returns will be equalized as factors migrate from low-return to high-return areas
Returns will be set by the central government to ensure regional equality
Returns will diverge over time as trade concentrates activity in coastal regions
questions.explanation: Factors move from low-return areas to high-return areas, and this migration process continues until all regional return differences are eliminated.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model, a country is partially specialized and produces both goods. What must be true about prices and unit labor requirements?
options: The domestic price ratio must be higher than the world price ratio for both goods
P_X / P_Y must exactly equal the ratio of unit labor requirements a_LX / a_LY
The wage in one sector must be higher than the wage in the other sector
The world price must be lower than the domestic autarky price for both goods
questions.explanation: For both sectors to coexist, wages must be equal across them, which only occurs when the price ratio matches the opportunity cost ratio.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, the world price of an export good rises. What happens to the return on capital in the import-competing sector?
options: It increases as the booming export sector lifts the entire domestic economy
It remains unchanged because capital is immobile and import prices are stable
It decreases as workers leave the import-competing sector for export industries
It initially rises but then falls as the export boom draws resources away
questions.explanation: Capital is stuck in the import-competing sector. Since the domestic price of imports is unchanged and factors cannot move, returns there are unaffected.

questions.question: Why does the immobile factor model suggest that trade may increase poverty in certain regions of a country?
options: Trade always reduces total national income in countries with immobile factors
Regions dependent on import-competing industries lose income with no path to transition
Foreign aid is redirected away from poor regions toward wealthy export-oriented areas
Immobile factors attract higher wages that then inflate the local cost of living
questions.explanation: If labor and capital cannot move to growing export hubs, those trapped in declining import-competing sectors suffer permanent income losses.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best example of an immobile factor within a six-month time horizon?
options: A software engineer who can work remotely for companies in any industry
A specialized blast furnace designed exclusively for steel mill production
A delivery truck that can transport goods for many different types of businesses
An office building that can be converted into retail or residential space
questions.explanation: Heavy industrial capital like a blast furnace cannot be converted or moved to another industry such as software within a short six-month period.

questions.question: An economy is labor-abundant according to the Heckscher-Ohlin definition. What does the model predict will happen to production when trade opens?
options: Production of capital-intensive goods will expand as trade increases investment
Production of labor-intensive goods will increase as the country specializes
Production of both goods will expand equally because trade boosts all sectors
Production will shift entirely toward services away from manufactured goods
questions.explanation: The H-O theorem predicts that countries specialize in and export goods that intensively use their relatively abundant factors of production.

questions.question: According to the H-O model, what happens to the relative price of the abundant factor when a country opens to trade?
options: It decreases because foreign competition drives down all domestic factor prices
It increases as demand rises for the good that uses the abundant factor intensively
It remains constant because factor endowments are fixed and cannot change
It initially falls in the short run but returns to the original level in the long run
questions.explanation: Trade expands the sector that uses the abundant factor intensively, increasing derived demand and raising the relative price of that factor.

questions.question: What is a major real-world criticism of the Factor Price Equalization theorem's predictions?
options: Trade volumes are too small in practice to have any measurable effect on wages
Significant technological differences between nations prevent full factor price convergence
Factor prices have actually been proven to equalize perfectly in all empirical studies
The theorem applies only to capital returns and makes no prediction about wages
questions.explanation: FPE assumes identical technology across countries. Real-world technology gaps allow wages to remain persistently different despite trade.

questions.question: How does the Heckscher-Ohlin model define factor abundance for a country?
options: By the total absolute quantity of each factor the country physically possesses
By the relative ratio of one factor to another compared to other countries
By the total dollar value of the country's capital stock and labor earnings
By the growth rate of the labor force relative to the capital stock over time
questions.explanation: Factor abundance is a relative measure, typically expressed as the capital-to-labor ratio or labor-to-capital ratio compared to trading partners.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, the price of Agricultural goods rises by 20%. What happens to the real rental rate of Land?
options: It increases by less than 20% because some gains go to the mobile factor
It increases by more than 20% due to the Magnification Effect on specific factors
It increases by exactly 20%, matching the agricultural price increase proportionally
It remains unchanged because Land is fixed in supply and cannot respond to prices
questions.explanation: The Magnification Effect ensures that the return to the specific factor in the sector with the rising price increases more than the price change itself.

questions.question: A technological improvement raises productivity of all Manufacturing workers by 10% in the Specific Factor Model. What happens to the equilibrium wage?
options: The wage rises by exactly 10%, matching the productivity increase proportionally
The wage increases, but by less than 10% as labor moves dampen the overall rise
The wage remains constant because technology only affects prices, not wages
The wage falls because higher productivity reduces the number of workers needed
questions.explanation: Labor demand in manufacturing shifts outward. Some labor moves from agriculture to manufacturing, which dampens the overall wage increase.

questions.question: Both goods prices (P_M and P_A) increase by exactly 5% in the Specific Factor Model. What happens to labor allocation between the two sectors?
options: Labor shifts entirely to manufacturing because those goods are more valuable
The allocation of labor between the two sectors remains exactly the same
Labor shifts to agriculture because food is a necessity with inelastic demand
Labor initially moves to manufacturing but returns to agriculture over time
questions.explanation: Both labor demand curves shift up by 5%. The equilibrium wage rises by 5%, but the relative incentive to switch sectors is zero.

questions.question: Why does the scarce specific factor in the import-competing sector lose from trade in the Specific Factor Model?
options: Trade increases the price of imported inputs used by that specific factor
The sector contracts and loses labor, reducing the marginal product of the specific factor
Foreign firms outbid domestic specific factor owners for the services of mobile labor
Government regulations require specific factor owners to compensate displaced workers
questions.explanation: Lower import prices cause the sector to contract. Labor leaves, and the remaining specific factor has fewer workers to assist, lowering its marginal productivity and return.

questions.question: Why does trade lead to lower consumer prices in the Krugman model of monopolistic competition?
options: Governments subsidize exports, allowing foreign firms to sell below cost
Firms exploit economies of scale by producing larger volumes for the global market
Trade eliminates all monopolies and forces perfect competition in every industry
Prices do not fall in the Krugman model; only product variety increases
questions.explanation: The larger integrated global market allows firms to produce at higher volumes, spreading fixed costs and lowering average costs and consumer prices.

questions.question: What is the variety gain from trade in New Trade Theory?
options: Firms produce more versions of each product to segment the domestic market
Consumers gain access to specialized foreign product varieties not made at home
Trade increases the total number of firms operating in each national economy
Governments mandate a minimum number of product varieties in each industry
questions.explanation: Trade expands the set of available varieties beyond domestic production, and consumers derive utility from having more choices.

questions.question: In an industry with external economies of scale, how might trade actually make a country worse off?
options: Trade always benefits all countries, so this scenario is theoretically impossible
The country may be forced into a low-growth sector while losing its scale-based industries
External economies disappear when trade opens, raising costs for all domestic firms
Trade causes all firms to relocate to the country with the lowest absolute wages
questions.explanation: Historical accidents and first-mover advantages can lock a country into low-productivity sectors while competitors capture high-scale industries elsewhere.

questions.question: A government provides a production subsidy to domestic producers of an import-competing good. What is the expected result?
options: Consumer prices rise because the subsidy is passed through to retail buyers
Domestic production increases and the volume of imports decreases
Imports increase because the subsidy makes foreign goods more attractive
Domestic production falls as firms become dependent on government support
questions.explanation: The subsidy lowers effective costs for domestic firms, allowing them to expand output and displace some imports without raising consumer prices.

questions.question: Which trade policy tool is most commonly justified by the infant industry argument?
options: Permanent import bans on all goods that compete with domestic production
Temporary tariffs or subsidies to help new firms achieve competitive scale
Export taxes that keep domestic raw materials cheap for local manufacturers
Voluntary export restraints negotiated with established foreign competitors
questions.explanation: The infant industry argument holds that new domestic industries need short-term protection until they can achieve economies of scale and compete globally.

questions.question: What is the practice of dumping in international trade?
options: Disposing of hazardous waste by shipping it to countries with weaker regulations
Selling a product abroad at a price below its cost of production or domestic price
Flooding a foreign market with so many goods that ports cannot handle the volume
Withdrawing all investments from a country suddenly during a financial crisis
questions.explanation: Dumping is selling exports at prices lower than the home market price or below production cost, considered an unfair trade practice.

questions.question: For national security purposes like maintaining food supply, why is a production subsidy preferred over an import quota?
options: Subsidies are always cheaper for the government to administer than quotas
Subsidies maintain domestic output without raising consumer food prices
Quotas are illegal under WTO rules while subsidies are always permitted
Quotas reduce domestic production while subsidies directly increase imports
questions.explanation: A production subsidy supports domestic farmers without restricting imports or raising consumer prices, avoiding the consumption distortion of a quota.

questions.question: A country's demand for a good is perfectly inelastic. What happens to the consumption distortion component of deadweight loss when a tariff is applied?
options: The consumption distortion is maximized because consumers still buy the good
The consumption distortion is zero because the quantity demanded does not change
The distortion equals the entire tariff revenue collected by the government
The distortion is split equally between consumers who buy and those who stop buying
questions.explanation: With perfectly inelastic demand, consumers purchase exactly the same quantity regardless of price, so no utility is lost from reduced consumption.

questions.question: In a large country case, who ultimately bears the cost of the terms-of-trade gain generated by a tariff?
options: Domestic consumers who must pay the higher tariff-inclusive price
Foreign exporters who accept lower pre-tariff prices to maintain sales
The domestic government through reduced income tax revenue elsewhere
Workers in the domestic import-competing sector through lower real wages
questions.explanation: The large country's tariff reduces global demand, forcing foreign exporters to lower their pre-tariff prices and absorb part of the tariff cost.

questions.question: What happens to domestic producer surplus when a government increases an existing tariff rate?
options: Producer surplus decreases because the higher tariff reduces total imports
Producer surplus increases as domestic firms sell more at a higher protected price
Producer surplus remains constant because it is determined by world prices only
Producer surplus initially rises but then falls as consumers reduce all purchases
questions.explanation: A higher tariff raises the domestic price. Domestic producers receive more per unit sold and expand production, increasing their surplus.

questions.question: What two components make up a compound tariff in international trade policy?
options: A tariff on raw materials combined with a separate tariff on finished goods
Both a specific per-unit tariff and an ad valorem percentage tariff together
A tariff applied during peacetime and an additional surcharge during wartime
An import tariff paired with an equivalent export subsidy for the same good
questions.explanation: A compound tariff combines a fixed dollar amount per unit (specific) with a percentage of the good's assessed value (ad valorem).

questions.question: How does the imposition of an import quota affect consumer surplus in the importing country?
options: Consumer surplus increases because domestic producers expand their output
Consumer surplus decreases because the restricted supply raises domestic prices
Consumer surplus remains unchanged because quotas only affect producer welfare
Consumer surplus initially rises as imports become higher quality under the quota
questions.explanation: By restricting the quantity of imports, the quota raises the domestic price. Consumers pay more and buy less, reducing their surplus.

questions.question: The government allocates import licenses to domestic firms based on their historical import volumes. Who captures the quota rents?
options: The government, because it collects fees from the firms that receive the licenses
The domestic firms receiving the licenses, buying at world price and selling at domestic price
Foreign exporters, because they can charge higher prices under the quota system
Consumers, because competition among license holders drives down retail prices
questions.explanation: License holders purchase goods at the low world price and resell at the higher domestic scarcity price, keeping the difference as quota rent.

questions.question: Which WTO principle requires that a country extend the same trade treatment to all of its trading partners equally?
options: National Treatment, which prevents discrimination against foreign goods domestically
Most-Favored Nation treatment, preventing discriminatory practices among member nations
Reciprocity, ensuring that tariff cuts are matched by trading partners equally
Transparency, requiring all trade policies to be publicly disclosed and reviewed
questions.explanation: The MFN principle means any trade advantage given to one WTO member must be extended to all other WTO members without discrimination.

questions.question: What is the primary danger of using anti-dumping duties as a trade policy instrument?
options: They are too weak to have any real effect on foreign pricing behavior
They can be misused as disguised protectionism against efficient foreign competitors
They always violate WTO rules and lead to immediate trade sanctions
They benefit only consumers while harming domestic producers and workers
questions.explanation: Firms may claim dumping simply because foreign competitors are more efficient and charge lower prices, using anti-dumping duties to block legitimate competition.

questions.question: What does a country's Net International Investment Position measure?
options: The annual flow of foreign direct investment into and out of the country
The stock of foreign assets owned minus the stock of liabilities owed to foreigners
The difference between the country's exports and imports of investment services
The total value of the central bank's official foreign currency reserve holdings
questions.explanation: NIIP is a stock measure capturing a country's external wealth at a point in time: foreign assets minus foreign liabilities.

questions.question: In which part of the Balance of Payments are interest and dividend earnings on foreign investments recorded?
options: The Financial Account, under the portfolio investment subcategory
The Current Account, specifically within the Primary Income component
The Capital Account, which records all non-financial asset transfers
The Official Reserves Account, managed by the central monetary authority
questions.explanation: Primary income is part of the Current Account and records returns on factors of production, including interest, dividends, and other investment earnings.

questions.question: A country has a Current Account surplus of \$50 billion and a Financial Account deficit of \$45 billion. What must Net Errors and Omissions equal?
options: +\$95 billion, reflecting the sum of the two account balances
–\$5 billion, to satisfy the identity CA + FA + E&O = 0
+\$5 billion, indicating unrecorded capital outflows from the country
\$0, because errors and omissions are always zero by definition
questions.explanation: CA + FA + E&O must equal zero. \$50B + (–\$45B) + E&O = 0, so E&O = –\$5 billion.

questions.question: What are official reserve assets held by a central bank?
options: The total value of all government-owned buildings and infrastructure abroad
Foreign currency, gold, and other assets used to intervene in exchange markets
The cumulative stock of all past current account surpluses and deficits
Domestic government bonds held by foreign central banks as investments
questions.explanation: Official reserve assets include foreign exchange, gold, and SDRs held by the central bank to manage the exchange rate and provide crisis liquidity.

questions.question: What does the Big Mac Index attempt to measure in international economics?
options: The relative labor cost of producing fast food in different countries
Whether currencies are overvalued or undervalued based on purchasing power parity
The impact of trade liberalization on global agricultural commodity prices
The relationship between fast food consumption and a country's GDP per capita
questions.explanation: The Big Mac Index uses a standardized product sold worldwide to compare purchasing power and assess currency valuation against PPP theory.

questions.question: How does a real appreciation of a nation's currency affect its net exports?
options: Net exports increase because domestic goods become cheaper for foreign buyers
Net exports decrease as domestic goods become more expensive for foreign buyers
Net exports remain unchanged because nominal rates matter more than real rates
Net exports fluctuate unpredictably depending on the composition of trade flows
questions.explanation: A real appreciation makes domestic goods costlier abroad and foreign goods cheaper at home, reducing exports and increasing imports, thus lowering net exports.

questions.question: In the Mundell-Fleming model with a fixed exchange rate, what is the effect of expansionary fiscal policy?
options: Output is unchanged because fiscal policy is completely ineffective under fixed rates
Output increases significantly because monetary policy must accommodate the fiscal expansion
Output decreases because higher government spending crowds out private investment
Output increases only temporarily before the exchange rate collapses under pressure
questions.explanation: Under fixed exchange rates, fiscal expansion causes the central bank to increase the money supply to maintain the peg, amplifying the output effect.

questions.question: What is exchange rate overshooting in international macroeconomics?
options: When a currency appreciates beyond its purchasing power parity level permanently
When the short-run exchange rate moves further than its long-run equilibrium after a shock
When central banks deliberately push the exchange rate beyond its market value
When the nominal rate changes but the real exchange rate remains unchanged
questions.explanation: Because goods prices are sticky but financial markets adjust instantly, the exchange rate must over-adjust in the short run to clear the money market.
